sentence_text: Acceleration is defined as any change in velocity, and velocity includes both speed and direction.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Even when an object moves in a circle at a constant speed, its direction changes continuously.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: At every point on the path, the velocity vector points in a new direction, tangent to the circle.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Because the direction changes, the velocity changes, which means the object is accelerating.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: This acceleration points inward toward the center of the circle and is called centripetal acceleration.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: By Newton’s second law, a net inward force must act to produce this acceleration, whether from tension, gravity, or friction.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: An object moving in a circle accelerates because acceleration means any change in velocity, and velocity includes both speed and direction.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Even if the speed stays the same, the direction is constantly changing as the object moves around the circle.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: At every instant, the object’s velocity points in a slightly new direction.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: That change in direction requires an inward acceleration called centripetal acceleration.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Without it, the object would move in a straight line instead.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: For example, when a car turns, the tires provide the inward force needed to keep changing the car’s direction, which is why turning creates acceleration.

sentence_text: This means a hammer and a feather would fall together in a vacuum.

sentence_text: Work in physics is defined as force times displacement times the cosine of the angle between them.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Work measures how much a force transfers energy in the direction of motion.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: If the force is perpendicular to the displacement, the angle is 90 degrees and the cosine is zero.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: That means no energy is transferred in the direction the object moves, so the work is zero.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: For example, gravity does no work on a bag carried horizontally because gravity acts downward while the bag moves sideways.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: A force can act on an object without doing work if it does not help move it along its path.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: Work is zero when force is perpendicular to displacement because work depends on the component of force in the direction of motion.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: The formula for work is force times displacement times the cosine of the angle between them.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: If the force is perpendicular, the angle is 90 degrees, and cosine of 90 degrees is zero.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: That means the force does not transfer energy into or out of the object’s motion along its path.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: For example, in uniform circular motion, the centripetal force points inward while motion is tangent to the circle, so the centripetal force does no work.

sentence_text: A seatbelt reduces injury because it increases the time over which your momentum changes during a sudden stop.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: The impulse-momentum theorem says that force times time equals the change in momentum.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: In a crash, your body must go from moving at car speed to rest either way.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: If that happens in a very short time, the force is extremely large.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: A seatbelt stretches slightly and spreads the stopping process over a longer time, reducing the peak force on your body.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: It also keeps you from hitting the dashboard or windshield, which greatly lowers injury risk.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: A truck is harder to stop than a bicycle moving at the same speed because the truck has much more momentum.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Momentum equals mass times velocity, so even at the same speed, the truck’s much larger mass gives it far greater momentum.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: To stop a moving object, you must reduce its momentum to zero, which requires an impulse.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: A larger momentum means you need more force, more time, or both to stop it.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: This is why trucks need stronger brakes and longer stopping distances.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: Greater mass also means greater inertia, which makes the truck resist changes in motion more strongly.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: A person standing still on ice still exerts a force on the surface because forces always come in pairs.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Your weight pulls downward due to gravity, so you push down on the ice.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: According to Newton’s third law, the ice pushes upward on you with an equal and opposite normal force.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: These two forces do not cancel because they act on different objects: one acts on you, and one acts on the ice.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: Even when you are standing still, you are continuously exerting force downward on the surface beneath you simply by making contact with it.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Friction does not always oppose motion itself; it opposes relative slipping between surfaces.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: When you walk, your foot pushes backward against the ground.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Static friction from the ground then pushes you forward, allowing you to move.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Without friction, your feet would slip and you could not walk effectively.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: The same idea explains how cars accelerate and turn: tires push against the road, and friction provides the forward or sideways force needed.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: Friction only directly resists motion when surfaces slide past each other.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: In many everyday situations, friction is what makes controlled motion possible.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: A rocket accelerates in space by pushing exhaust gases backward at high speed.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: By Newton’s third law, every action force has an equal and opposite reaction force.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: The rocket engine burns fuel and expels hot gas out of the nozzle.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: As the rocket pushes the gas backward, the gas pushes the rocket forward with an equal and opposite force called thrust.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: A rocket does not need air to push against because it carries its own fuel and propellant.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: This is why rockets work in the vacuum of space and can maneuver far from Earth’s atmosphere.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: A battery stores chemical potential energy and converts it into electrical energy through chemical reactions.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: When the circuit is closed, these reactions move charge and maintain a voltage difference between the battery terminals.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: As current flows, the reactants inside the battery are gradually used up.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Over time, the battery can no longer sustain the same chemical reactions, so the voltage drops and the battery eventually runs out.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Some energy is also lost as heat because of the battery’s internal resistance.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: Rechargeable batteries work by reversing the chemical reactions using an external power source to restore the stored energy.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: A spark happens when excess electric charge builds up and creates a strong electric field across a small gap of air.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Air is normally an insulator, but if the electric field becomes strong enough, it can ionize the air molecules.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: This strips electrons from atoms and makes the air temporarily conductive.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: The stored charge then suddenly rushes across the gap as a brief electric current, producing a spark, a flash of light, and a snapping sound.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Static electricity from walking on carpet often causes this effect.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: Lightning is the same basic process, but on a much larger scale in the atmosphere.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: A compass needle is a small bar magnet that can rotate freely.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Earth behaves like a giant magnet and produces a magnetic field around the planet.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: When the compass is placed in this field, the needle experiences a torque that causes it to rotate.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: The needle aligns itself with the magnetic field lines because that is the most stable orientation.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: This makes the needle point roughly north-south.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: The compass works because magnetic fields exert forces and torques on other magnets.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: That simple interaction makes the compass a reliable tool for navigation.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: A changing magnetic field through a wire loop changes the magnetic flux through the loop.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Faraday’s law says that a changing magnetic flux induces an electromotive force, which is like a voltage around the loop.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: If the loop is made of conducting wire, this induced voltage drives a current.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: A steady magnetic field, even a strong one, does not create current by itself.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: Only change matters.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: This principle is used in electric generators, transformers, induction stoves, and wireless chargers.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: Changing magnetic fields are one of the main ways electricity can be produced without direct contact.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: In a series circuit, there is only one path for electric charge to flow.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Current is the amount of charge passing a point each second.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Because charge is conserved, the same amount of charge must pass every point in the circuit in the same amount of time.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: If current were different in different parts of the circuit, charge would have to build up somewhere, which does not happen in a steady circuit.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: This is why the current is the same through every component in a series circuit, even though the voltage may change across different parts.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Voltage is electric potential energy per unit charge.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: As current moves through a resistor, bulb, or motor, some of that electrical energy is converted into other forms such as heat, light, or motion.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: That means the charge leaves the component with less energy than it had before, so the voltage drops across the component.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: In a complete loop, energy is conserved: the total energy supplied by the battery equals the total energy used by all the components.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: This is why the voltage drops across each part of a circuit add up to the battery’s total voltage.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Two neutral objects can still attract electrically because charges inside them can shift.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: A nearby charged object causes the positive and negative charges in a neutral object to rearrange slightly, a process called polarization.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: The side closer to the charged object becomes slightly opposite in charge, while the far side becomes slightly like-charged.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Because electric force gets weaker with distance, the closer attractive force is stronger than the farther repulsive force.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: This creates a net attraction even though the object is still overall neutral.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: This is why a charged balloon can stick to a wall.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: Rubbing your hands together creates friction between your skin surfaces.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: The mechanical work you do against friction is converted into thermal energy.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: On a microscopic level, friction increases the random motion of molecules in your skin, which raises the temperature.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Your nerves detect this increase as warmth.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: This is a common example of mechanical energy turning into heat.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: The same principle explains why brakes get hot, why a drill bit heats up, and why sliding objects can warm up due to friction.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: Energy is not lost; it is simply transformed into a different form.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: Temperature is related to the average kinetic energy of gas molecules.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: When a sealed container is heated, the gas molecules move faster on average.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Faster molecules hit the walls more often and with greater force.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Since the volume of the container stays fixed, the gas cannot expand to relieve the extra motion.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: As a result, the pressure rises.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: This relationship is part of the ideal gas law and explains everyday effects like rising tire pressure after driving or the danger of heating sealed aerosol cans.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: More energetic molecular collisions directly create higher pressure.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: A hot drink cools faster at first because the temperature difference between the drink and the surrounding air is largest initially.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Heat transfer happens more quickly when this temperature difference is large.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: As the drink cools, the difference becomes smaller, so heat flows out more slowly.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: This is described by Newton’s law of cooling, which says the rate of heat loss is proportional to the temperature difference.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: That is why the temperature drops quickly at first and then levels off more gradually as the drink approaches room temperature.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: A battery eventually runs out because its stored chemical energy is gradually converted into electrical energy.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Inside the battery, chemical reactions separate charges and create a voltage difference between the terminals.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: When the circuit is closed, charges flow and the reactions continue.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Over time, the chemicals that drive these reactions are used up or become less effective.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: As this happens, the battery can no longer maintain the same voltage, so current decreases.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: A closed circuit allows energy transfer, but it does not mean energy is endlessly recycled.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: The battery is the energy source, and that source is limited.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: Touching a charged object sometimes produces a spark because electric charge can suddenly jump through the air.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: If there is a large enough voltage difference between your body and the object, electrons move quickly to balance the charges.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Normally, air acts as an insulator, but a strong electric field can ionize the air, allowing current to flow briefly.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: That rapid discharge creates light, heat, and the tiny crackling sound of a spark.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Dry air makes this more common because charge builds up more easily.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: The spark is simply the fast release of stored electrical energy between two differently charged objects.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: A compass needle aligns with Earth’s magnetic field because the needle is a small magnet.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Magnets experience a torque in a magnetic field that causes them to rotate until they line up with the field direction.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Earth acts like a giant magnet, producing a magnetic field that extends through space around it.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: When the compass is free to rotate, the magnetic forces on its poles make it settle in the north-south direction.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: The needle is not being pulled north by location alone.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: Instead, it is responding to the magnetic field at that point on Earth’s surface.

sentence_text: A changing magnetic field induces current in a wire loop because changing magnetic fields create electric fields.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: This is described by Faraday’s law.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: When the magnetic field through a loop changes, the charges in the wire feel a force that makes them move, producing current.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: The faster the magnetic field changes, the greater the induced current.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: This principle is used in generators, transformers, and wireless charging.

sentence_text: The key idea is that electricity and magnetism are linked.

sentence_text: Current is the same everywhere in a series circuit because there is only one path for charges to follow.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Charge cannot pile up in one part of the circuit while disappearing in another.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: In a steady circuit, the same amount of charge that enters each component must leave it.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: If current were different at different points, charge would build up somewhere, which does not happen in normal operation.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: You can think of it like water flowing through a single pipe loop: the same amount of water passes every point each second.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: In a series circuit, all components share the same charge flow rate.

sentence_text: A seatbelt reduces injury during a sudden stop by increasing the time over which your body comes to rest.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Without a seatbelt, your body would keep moving forward at the car’s original speed because of inertia, possibly hitting the dashboard or windshield.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: A seatbelt applies a restraining force that slows you down more gradually.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Since force depends on how quickly momentum changes, spreading the stop over a longer time reduces the force on your body.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Seatbelts also keep you in the proper position so airbags can work effectively.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: They prevent you from being thrown forward, which greatly lowers the chance of serious injury.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: It is harder to stop a truck than a bicycle moving at the same speed because the truck has much more mass and therefore much more momentum.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Momentum is the product of mass and velocity.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Since both are moving at the same speed, the truck’s larger mass means it carries far more momentum.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: To stop it, a larger force or more stopping time is needed.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: The truck also has greater kinetic energy, which must be removed by brakes and friction.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: That is why trucks need longer stopping distances and stronger braking systems than bicycles, even when traveling at the same speed.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: A person standing still on ice can still exert a force on the surface because force does not require motion.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Even when standing still, the person’s weight pulls downward due to gravity.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: This downward force pushes on the ice.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: According to Newton’s third law, the ice pushes back upward with an equal and opposite force called the normal force.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: These forces balance, so the person remains at rest.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: The fact that nothing moves does not mean no forces are present.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: It only means the forces are balanced.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: Standing still is actually a situation where forces are acting but canceling out.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: Friction can help motion because it provides the grip needed to push against a surface.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: Although friction often opposes sliding, it also prevents unwanted slipping.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: For example, when you walk, your foot pushes backward on the ground.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: Static friction from the ground then pushes you forward.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: Without friction, your foot would slide and you could not move effectively.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: The same idea applies to car tires, which rely on friction to accelerate, turn, and brake.
label: INTUITION

sentence_text: So friction is not always a force that slows things down.
label: CAVEAT

sentence_text: In many situations, it is essential for controlled movement and stability.
label: VERIFY

sentence_text: A rocket accelerates in space because it pushes mass out of its engines at high speed.
label: FRAME

sentence_text: According to Newton’s third law, when the rocket pushes exhaust gases backward, the gases push the rocket forward with an equal and opposite force.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: The rocket does not need air to push against.

sentence_text: It only needs to eject mass.
label: PRINCIPLE_DERIVE

sentence_text: This is why rockets work in the vacuum of space.

sentence_text: The force comes from the internal combustion and expulsion of fuel, not from interaction with the atmosphere.

sentence_text: As long as the rocket continues throwing exhaust backward, it can keep accelerating forward.

label: FRAME

sentence_text: When the drink is very hot, the difference is large, so heat flows out quickly through conduction, convection, and radiation.

sentence_text: As the drink cools, the temperature difference becomes smaller, so heat transfer slows down.

sentence_text: This is why the first few minutes cause the biggest temperature drop.

sentence_text: The cooling process naturally becomes slower over time.

sentence_text: In simple terms, the farther the drink is from room temperature, the faster it loses heat.
